coredata.dc:title: A selective off–on fluorescent aptasensor for alpha-fetoprotein determination based on N-carbon quantum dots and oxidized nanocellulose
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Afsharipour, Roya
Haji Shabani, Ali Mohammad
Dadfarnia, Shayessteh
coredata.dc:description: A selective off–on aptasensor was designed to measure tumor marker alpha-fetoprotein (AFP). Nitrogen carbon quantum dots modified with aptamer was synthesized. The addition of oxidized nanocellulose to this system causes quenching of the fluorescence intensity. But in the presence of AFP, the aptamers bind strongly with AFP, resulting in the release of oxidized nanocellulose and an increase in the fluorescence intensity of Apt@N-CQDs. The important parameters on AFP determination were optimized through response surface methodology and central composite design. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity enhanced linearly with AFP concentration in the range of 0.03–7.0 µg L−1 with a detection limit of 0.008 µg L−1 and relative standard deviations of 2.2 and 3.8% (at 4.0 µg L−1 of AFP; for n = 6) for intra- and inter-day assays. The method was successfully applied for the determination of AFP in human serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tumor markers
Aptasensor
Alpha-fetoprotein
Fluorescence intensity
Oxidized nanocellulose

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nanocelluloses and oxidized nanocelluloses
Comparison of designed aptasensor with other methods

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of AFP measurement using Apt@N-CQDs as a fluorescent nanoprobe and oxidized nanocellulose as a quencher.
FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs (a), Apt@N-CQDs (b), oxidized nanocellulose (c).
UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CQDs (a), and fluorescence emission spectrum of NCQDs (b).
The normal probability plot for the predicted data versus the experimental results.

3-D graphs for the AFP measurement. The time of Interaction between NCs and Apt@N-CQDs and concentration of NCs (a), Interaction time between AFP and Apt@N-CQDs and concentration of NCs (b), and interaction time between AFP and Apt@N-CQDs and NCs and Apt@N-CQDs (c).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra responses of the proposed aptasensor to various concentrations of the AFP, (b) Calibration plot of the proposed aptasensor at various concentrations of the AFP (0.03–7.0 µg L−1) at excitation wavelengths of 200–400 nm.
Comparison of the analytical signal of designed aptasensor for AFP at 2.0 μg L−1 and other species at 200.0 μg L−1 levels.
Variables and their levels in central composite design with experimental results for the measurement of AFP.

row.entry: (X1) Concentration of NCs (mg mL−1), (X2) Time between NCs and Apt@NCQDs (min), (X3) Time between AFP and Apt@NCQDs (min),

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of AFP in real samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *Mean and standard deviation of three independent analyses.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the designed method with other methods of determination of AFP.

head.ce:title: A selective off–on fluorescent aptasensor for alpha-fetoprotein determination based on N-carbon quantum dots and oxidized nanocellulose

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Yazd University, Yazd, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Yazd University, Yazd, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A selective off–on aptasensor was designed to measure tumor marker alpha-fetoprotein (AFP). Nitrogen carbon quantum dots modified with aptamer was synthesized. The addition of oxidized nanocellulose to this system causes quenching of the fluorescence intensity. But in the presence of AFP, the aptamers bind strongly with AFP, resulting in the release of oxidized nanocellulose and an increase in the fluorescence intensity of Apt@N-CQDs. The important parameters on AFP determination were optimized through response surface methodology and central composite design. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity enhanced linearly with AFP concentration in the range of 0.03–7.0 µg L−1 with a detection limit of 0.008 µg L−1 and relative standard deviations of 2.2 and 3.8% (at 4.0 µg L−1 of AFP; for n = 6) for intra- and inter-day assays. The method was successfully applied for the determination of AFP in human serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Alpha-fetoprotein is one of the essential and main proteins that is produced in the yolk sac, gastrointestinal tract, and liver of the fetus during the fetal period and flows into the blood of the mother and fetus after being made [1,2]. Usually, this type of protein is not present or is found at a very low amount (less than 40 µg L−1) in the blood of healthy men with non-liver cancer and non-pregnant women [2]. However, in the blood serum of pregnant women, the concentration level of this protein varies depending on factors such as weight, race, diabetes status (ie, whether a person needs insulin injections), and the age of the fetus. Generally, its value at the beginning of pregnancy is about 0.2 µg L−1 and reaches up to 250 µg L−1 in the thirty-second week. Thus, depending on the age of the fetus, abnormal changes in the level of this protein in the mother's blood serum can be a sign of diseases such as spinal canal blockage, down syndrome (trisomy 21), or omphalocele (a type of defect in the abdominal wall) and encephalitis [2]. Therefore, for early detection of these diseases, measuring its amount in the blood serum of a pregnant woman with a selective and sensitive method is necessary. Different techniques have been reported for AFP detection including chemiluminescence and electrochemiluminescence techniques [3], electrochemical bioassays [4], enzymes, lateral flow immunoassay [5,6], and fluorescent biosensor [7–9]. Among the mentioned techniques, fluorescence sensors have more considered because of the simplicity of the instrument. To increase the selectivity of these sensors, molecular imprinted polymer or antibodies are often employed [10–14]. For example, in 2016, Karfa et al. synthesized a fluorescent molecular imprinted polymer for the measurement of AFP [8]. They immobilized molecularly imprinted polymer on the carbon quantum dots modified with vinyl silane as fluorescent support. The fluorescent intensity of the designed system was increased proportionally to AFP concentration. A detection limit of 0.42 ng mL−1 for the quantification of AFP was reported. In 2019, Sun et al. presented a fluorescent nanosensor based on carbon dots modified with molecularly imprinted polymer for selective determination of AFP in clinical samples [9]. The decrease in the fluorescence intensity of this nanosensor in presence of AFP was used as the analytical signal. The designed method with a detection limit of 0.474 ng mL−1 was employed for the determination of the AFP in serum samples. In 2020, Chen et al. designed an immunosensor fluorescent for the determination of AFP based on CdTe quantum dots and copper ion labeled antibodies [15]. For this purpose, the surface of the antibody was decorated with copper ions using a cross-linking reagent. In the absence of AFP, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe quantum dots decreases due to its interaction with antibody decorated with copper ions, but upon addition of AFP, its selective interaction with antibody resulted in the release of CdTe quantum dots and an increase in the fluorescent intensity of the system proportional the analyte concentration. A detection limit of 26 ng mL−1 was reported.

Among fluorimetric methods, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) has received more attention due to its simplicity and high sensitivity [16]. This technique uses a pair of donor and acceptor species that when donor fluorescence species is near the acceptor, its fluorescence intensity decreases which is employed as an analytical signal for monitoring the concentrations of the target [17,18]. So far, many researchers have reported the use of this technique for the sensitive detection of various tumor markers, but there is no report for AFP [19,20]. Furthermore, in the design of the FRET system as a sensing platform, different nanomaterials including carbon nanotubes, gold nanoparticles, metal–organic frameworks, MoS2 nanosheet, and graphene oxide have been employed as an energy and electron acceptor that reduce the decrease of the fluorescent intensity [21–23]. However, on the literature survey, we did not find any report on the use of the oxidized nanocellulose as an electron acceptor and fluorescence intensity reducer. Nanocelluloses (NCs) produced from cellulose as a natural biopolymer have applications in various fields due to their unique properties like biocompatibility, surface reactivity, availability, low density, and cytotoxicity. Some features of nanocellulose such as its morphology, surface chemistry, aspect ratio, crystal structure, and the amount of crystallinity are related to the source of the cellulose, separation method, and the physicochemical pretreatment for the separation process [24].

Aptamers are single-stranded DNA or RNA with a unique three-dimensional structure that has an excellent affinity to bind specifically to the target molecules such as proteins, small organic molecules, and microorganisms [25–29]. Furthermore, in comparison with the antibodies, aptamers have the advantages of facile synthesis and chemical modification, robust chemical stability, cost-effectiveness, small size, and simple storage and transportation [30–35]. Aptamers also possess the capability of modification with various functional groups [36,37], biomolecules [38], and fluorescence particles [25] resulting in the construction of the more flexible sensor as well as easy detection of the target analytes. Carbon quantum dots modified with aptamer along with graphene oxide as a quencher have been utilized for measurement of different species, but so far there is no report on its use for AFP determination.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received enormous attention especially in the fabrication of sensors due to their excellent photostability, good photoluminescence, great biocompatibility, and low toxicity [39–41]. One of the drawbacks of CQDs is the low fluorescence quantum yield that has been overcome by its surface passivation with various organic molecules [42,43] or doping with heteroatoms such as nitrogen and sulfur [44,45]. Thus, based on the above discussion, in this study nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot was synthesized and its surface modified with aptamer (Apt@N-CQDs). Then, a selective off–on FRET nanosensor based on Apt@N-CQDs and oxidized nanocellulose as quencher was designed for the direct determination of AFP. After characterization of the structure of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and Apt@N-CQDs, the effect of various variables on the quantification of AFP was optimized by the surface response methodology based on the central composite design.

Sodium citrate, ethylenediamine, phenylalanine, L-serin, glycine, asparagine, and tyrosine were obtained from Merk Company (Darmstadt, Germany). The aptamer with sequences of 5′-NH2GGCAGGAAGACAAACAAGCTTGGCGGCGGGAAGGTGTTTAAATTCCCGGGTCTGCGTGGTCTGTGGTGCTGT-3′ was prepared from Bioneer Company (South Korea). Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. A standard solution of Apt (100 µg L−1) was prepared and kept at 4 °C in a refrigerator.

The fluorescence intensity was recorded using a fluorescence array spectrophotometer (Teifsanje Tajhiz Pishraftheh Co. Ltd., model FL-Ar, Iran) equipped with the deuterium/tungsten excitation. A UV–Vis spectrophotometer model SPECORD 250 (Analytik Jena AG, Germany) was utilized for monitoring the UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-CQDs. A Brucker vector 22 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrophotometer (Bruker Equinox 55, Germany) was employed for recording FT-IR spectra. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out by an X-ray diffractometer model X’Pert Pro (Panalytical, Netherlands). A Zeiss-EM10C electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 100 kV (Zeiss, Germany) was used for obtaining transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. A ZEISS Sigma VP FESEM with a mapping detector (ZEISS, Germany) was applied for recording the field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM).

N-CQDs was prepared according to the previous reporting procedure with some modification [46]. 1.26 g of sodium citrate as carbon source and 6.0 mL of ethylenediamine as nitrogen source were dissolved in 30 mL of distilled water and transferred to a Teflon autoclave (50 mL). Then, the autoclave was heated at 190 °C for 7 h. Finally, the orange solution formed was kept at 4 °C in a refrigerator.

Nitrogen carbon quantum dots contain carboxylic groups that can be activated with NHS and EDC to interact with the amine groups of the aptamer. Thus, 0.5 mL of N-CQDs was dissolved in 10.0 mL of phosphate buffer (pH = 7.3) and 1.2 mL of EDC/NHS with the concentration ratio of 8/2 (mM) was added to the flask and stirred for 45 min. Then, 300 µL of 0.1 M AFP aptamer solution was added and stirred for 22 h at room temperature. In this step, an amide bond is formed between the NH2 group of the aptamer and the activated groups on the surface of N-CQDs. In the next stage, the mixture was centrifuged at a speed of 8000 rpm for 15 min, the N-CQDs modified with aptamer were collected and washed three times with phosphate buffer to remove the excess NHS and EDC. The resulting product was dispersed in the phosphate buffer by vortex and stored in the refrigerator for later uses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nanocelluloses and oxidized nanocelluloses
ce:section.ce:para: Nanocelluloses (NCs) were prepared by acid hydrolysis of cotton. Thus, 13.0 g of cotton fiber was added to 400.0 mL of NaOH (2.0 M) and mixed with a mechanical stirrer at 70 °C for 24.0 h to remove protein, lignin, and hemicellulose from it. The product was then washed with distilled water to neutral pH and dried in an oven at 50 °C [47]. In the next step, 10 mL of 40% sulfuric acid solution was added to the prepared cotton and the resulting mixture was stirred for 1.0 h at 50 °C. The surface of the resulting nanocellulose is full of the hydroxyl group. To oxidize the NCs and convert some of its OH groups to carboxylic acid, 0.5 g of the prepared NCs was added to 10.0 mL of potassium persulfate (0.5 M) and stirred for 16.0 h at 75 °C. Finally, the oxidized nanocelluloses were washed thoroughly with distilled water and dried at room temperature [24].

For determination of AFP, 300.0 µL of Apt@N-CQDs, 50.0 µL of oxidized NCs (3.0 mg mL), along with 100.0 µL of phosphate buffer (0.2 M, pH = 7.0) was transferred to an Eppendorf tube (1.5 mL), vortexed for 1.0 min and allowed to stand for 40 min at room temperature for completion of its interaction with oxidized NCs. Then, the quenched fluorescence intensity of Apt@N-CQDs at 440 nm was recorded (F0). In the next step, 150.0 µL standard or sample solution of AFP containing not more than 1.0 mg of analyte was added to the Apt@N-CQDs solution, incubated for 22 min at room temperature, and its fluorescence at 440 nm was measured (F). The relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0-1) (where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of Apt@N-CQDs in the presence and absence of AFP) was utilized as an analytical signal for the determination of AFP concentration. The schematic of the determination of AFP is given in Fig. 1 .

The serum sample (2.0 mL) was provided by a patient suspected of liver cancer (Razi laboratory of Yazd) and was frozen at −20 °C before use. The sample was thawed at room temperature, 1.0 mL of acetonitrile was added and centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 15 min to precipitate its proteins [48]. The supernatant was then diluted with distilled water in a ratio in which the concentration of alpha-fetoprotein was in the linear range of the calibration graph, and finally, the AFP was measured according to the procedure presented in Section 5.2.

The preliminary experiment established that N-CQDs modified with aptamer through the formation of the bond between the amine groups of aptamer and activated carboxylic groups of nitrogen carbon quantum dots increases the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs. Furthermore, the addition of the biocompatible oxidized nanocellulose to the aptamer-modified N-CQDs quenched its fluorescence because of the formation of the hydrogen bonds between them. However, the addition of AFP to this system causes the retrieval of the fluorescence signal (turn on) due to the strong interaction between aptamer and AFP and release of the oxidize nanocellulose. The (F/F0-1) (where F0 is the quenched fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs in the presence of oxidized nanocellulose and F indicates the retrieve fluorescence of the system in the presence of AFP) was found to be proportional to the concentration of AFP and used as the analytical signal. These observations led us to design a fluorescence system based on the nitrogen-doped quantum dots modified with aptamer for selective determination of AFP. After the investigation of the structure of N-CQDs and Apt@N-CQDs, the effective parameters on the determination of AFP were optimized and the capability of designed aptasensor for determining the AFP in human serum samples was investigated.
The features of synthesized N-CQDs and Apt@N-CQDs were investigated by FT-IR, TEM, UV–Vis, and fluorescence spectrophotometry.

As shown in Fig. 2 b, the broad band at 3000–3500 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H respectively, demonstrating the presence of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of N-CQDs and Apt@N-CQDs. The peak at 1601 and 1717 cm−1 is ascribed to the vibration bands of CO in COOH and CONH, respectively. The absorption bands at 1701 and 1391 cm−1 are due to the CO stretching. The existence of the absorption band at 1100 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of P-O of aptamer, confirming the labeling of N-CQDs with AFP aptamer.

The morphology and particle size of the N-CQDs were studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM image exhibited that the prepared N-CQDs are nearly spherical with an average size in the range of 5–7 nm (Fig. 3 ).

The optical properties of N-CQDs were also evaluated by UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrophotometry. The UV–Vis spectrum of the synthesized N-CQDs (Fig. 4 a) shows two absorption bands, one at 260 nm due to the π-π* transition of the aromatic C = C and one at 350 nm related to n-π* transition of CO. The fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs and Apt@N-CQDs (Fig. 4b) show a strong fluorescence emission in the 320–720 nm with a maximum of 440 nm. The enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of Apt@N-CQDs in comparison to N-CQDs is due to the increase in its volume and reduction of its mobility.

To achieve the best conditions and highest sensitivity in the determination of AFP as well as considering the interactions between variables and reducing the number of experiments, optimization was performed by the central composite design (CCD) method. Based on the preliminary experiments, the effects of variables of nanocellulose concentration, NCs interaction time with Apt@N-CQDs, and the interaction time of alpha-fetoprotein with Apt@N-CQDs, which had the greatest effect on the analytical signal at optimum pH of 7.0 (Fig. S1), was optimized by designing a central composite experiment. The CCD includes three classes, full, ½ fraction and small, in which in the full class, each parameter is evaluated at five levels (±α, ±1, 0) to decrease the uncontrollable effects. The values and levels of each variable using the full class of CCD are given in Table 1 . Based on the equation of N = 2 K + 2 K + No (where K is the number of parameters and No is the number of central points) a total number of 20 experiments (Table 1) were predicted and performed. Experiments were designed randomly to avoid the effect of uncontrolled variables and to reduce systematic error. The resulting data were then modeled and analyzed using design expert software version 11/05.

In regression models, the appropriate model can be identified and evaluated by analyzing the total variance relative to the model variance and error variance. In the method of analysis of variance (ANOVA), the Fisher test (F-test) is used for statistical analysis of the results. The designed experiment was performed and the ANOVA results are presented in Table S1. A P value less than 0.05 indicates the significance of the variables at the 95% confidence level, an F value greater than the tabulated F is an indication of the significance of the expressions in the quadratic equation at the 95% confidence level whereas, a LOF value more than 0.05 shows the fit of experimental data with the model date.

The values of R2, adjusted R2 and predicted R2, lack of fit (LOF > 0.05), and adequate precision were calculated to evaluate the quality of the resulting polynomial. The second-order variance model was found as the best model for evaluating the experimental data. Based on the ANOVA table, the following quadratic polynomial equation with the lowest p-value as well as R2, adjusted R2, and predicted R2 closer to one (Table S2), was best fitted to the experimental results.
R = + 0.11 - 0.0073
X 1

X

2

The superiority of the obtained model is determined by evaluating the normal probability curve, the residual curves versus the test number, and the predicted value curve versus the experimental values. The normal curve of the residual values obtained from the difference between the predicted and experimental values (Fig. S2) indicates the normality of the data and the appropriateness of the model. The plot of residual values versus the test number (Fig. S3) shows that the data distribution is completely random and does not follow a specific pattern that confirms the appropriateness of the selected model. The graph of the predicted values versus the experimental values (Fig. 5 ) also shows a good correlation between the responses and the predicted values and confirms the good desirability of the model.

The 3D response plots were used to investigate the interaction among parameters, obtaining the maximum response and optimum value for each parameter (Fig. 6 ). The results of this investigation (Fig. 6a, b) revealed that 50 µL of NCs with a concentration of 0.3 mg mL−1 is enough for complete interaction with Apt@N-CQDs. The graph of analytical signal versus NCs concentration and time of interaction of NCs with Apt@N-CQDs (Fig. 6a–c) shows that the analytical signal reaches its maximum value after 40 min and a further increase in the interaction time has no significant effect on the analytical signal. Thus, the interaction of NCs with Apt@N-CQDs is completed after 40 min and maximum quenching of fluorescence is achieved. Fig. 6b and c indicate that the analytical signal increases with increasing interaction time between alpha-fetoprotein and Apt@N-CQDs up to 22 min, but remains constant after that. In other words, for the desorption of nanocellulose from Apt@N-CQDs and its replacement with the alpha-fetoprotein, at least 22 min is required. Therefore, 22 min was chosen as the optimal time.

To confirm the claimed optimized conditions by CCD, the control experiments were performed (Table S2). For this purpose, the theoretical response at the AFP concentration level of 4.0 µg L−1 was compared with the experimental results under optimum conditions. The mean amount of experimental response was found to be 0.1103 ± 0.0024 that at95% confidence level (n = ) it is in agreement with the claimed theoretical response of 0.1120 by CCD (t experimental = 1.58, t critical = 2.78).

Under the optimized condition, the figures of merit of developed aptasensor were investigated. The fluorescence emission spectra of the Apt@N-CQDs (Fig. 7 b) show that the fluorescence intensity increased with increasing the concentration of APT in the range of 0.03–7.0 µg L−1. The regression equation and correlation coefficient were found to be y = 0.0249C + 0.0158 and 0.9924, respectively (where y is the retrieve fluorescence (F/F0 – 1) and C is the concentration of AFP (μg L−1)). The detection limit (DL) and quantification limit (QL) calculated as 3sb/m and 10sb/m (sb is the standard deviation of the blank and m is the slope of the calibration curve) were 0.0075 and 0.025 µg L−1, respectively. The relative standard deviation of intra- and inter-day determinations (n = 6) of AFP at 4.0 µg L−1 was found to be 2.2 and 3.8%, respectively.

The selectivity of the designed method for measuring alpha-fetoprotein was evaluated by considering the analytical signal of some species present in the blood serum such as the amino acids of phenylalanine, asparagine, glycine, L-serine, and tyrosine, sodium and potassium ions, and alpha-fetoprotein at the concentration of 200.0 µg L−1 under the optimized experimental conditions.

The results of this study (Fig. 8 ) illustrate that the analytical signal of these species can be ignored in comparison with the AFP signal at a concentration level of 2.0 µg L−1. Thus, the designed aptasensor is not suitable for measuring these species and the method possesses good selectivity.

The ability of the developed method for the measurement of AFP in complex matrices of blood serum samples was evaluated. The accuracy of the method was studied by calculating the recovery percentage of added AFP at two concentration levels to the samples and comparing the results with the data obtained from the independent method of enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). The results of this study are reported in Table 2 and show the recovery of added AFP is quantitative (95–105%) and at the 95% confidence level, the calculated t (2.50 and 1.30) is less than the tabulated value of 4.30 which indicates that there is no significant difference between the values ​​obtained with the designed method and the ELISA method. Thus, the lack of effect of the sample matrices on the measurement of AFP as well as good accuracy of the method is confirmed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of designed aptasensor with other methods
ce:section.ce:para: The figures of merit of the developed method for measuring AFP were compared with other reported methods. The result (Table 3 ) confirms that the developed method based on the Apt@N-CQDs and oxidized NCs possess a good detection limit with a linear range comparable to other methods. In addition, the proposed method is a simple method with high selectivity that uses biocompatible materials and inexpensive equipment without having the limitation of pH sensitivity of the antigen–antibody-based immunosensor methods or the complex steps of the synthesis of the molecularly imprinted polymer.

Herein, a selective, sensitive, and environmental-friendly off–on aptasensor for the determination of trace level of AFP based on the N-CQDs modified with aptamer and oxidized NCs was developed. The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs increases upon modification with aptamer and decreases in the presence of oxidized NCs. The addition of AFP and its selective interaction with aptamer resulted in the release of oxidized NCs and retrieval of the fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, in this study, oxidized NCs, a biocompatible compound, was introduced as a fluorescence quencher of the N-CQDs modified with aptamer. The effective parameters on the measurement of AFP were optimized using the surface response methodology and central composite design. The developed aptasensor with high selectivity and the low detection limit was applied for the determination of trace levels of AFP in human blood serum and urine samples.

Roya Afsharipour: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Ali Mohammad Haji Shabani: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Shayessteh Dadfarnia: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2022.113872.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitive label-free immunosensor for detection alpha-fetoprotein in whole blood based on anticoagulating magnetic nanoparticles
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Xu, B. Chi, F. Wu, S. Ma, S. Zhan, M. Yi, H. Xu, C. Mao, A sensitive label-free immunosensor for detection alpha-fetoprotein in whole blood based on anticoagulating magnetic nanoparticles, Biosens. Bioelectron. 95 (2017) 87–93.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of a microfluidic paper-based bioimmunosensor with laser-induced fluorescence detection in the determination of alpha-fetoprotein from serum of hepatopaths
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Wang, X. Cai, Q. Li, L. Zheng, X. Yu, H. Zhang, J. Wang, Application of a microfluidic paper-based bioimmunosensor with laser-induced fluorescence detection in the determination of alpha-fetoprotein from serum of hepatopaths, Talanta 221 (2021) 121660.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical immunosensor for α-fetoprotein detection using ferroferric oxide and horseradish peroxidase as signal amplification labels
Anal. Biochem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Li, Q. Guo, W. Cao, Y. Li, B. Du, Q. Wei, Electrochemical immunosensor for α-fetoprotein detection using ferroferric oxide and horseradish peroxidase as signal amplification labels, Anal. Biochem .457 (2014) 59-64.

Anal. Chim. Acta

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new enzyme immunoassay for alpha-fetoprotein in a separate setup coupling an aluminium/Prussian blue-based self-powered electrochromic display with a digital multimeter readout
Analyst

Microchim. Acta
